pattern: Fixing where the left part ends turns both split constraints into prefix-sum thresholds on where the mid part can end, and because every value is non-negative the prefix sums are non-decreasing, so the valid range of mid-endings only ever slides forward as the left-ending slides forward -- a two-pointer sweep counts every good split in one pass.
hints: Work with prefix sums. If the left part ends at index i (1-indexed length i) and the mid part ends at index j, then leftSum = prefix[i], midSum = prefix[j]-prefix[i], rightSum = prefix[n]-prefix[j]; you need leftSum <= midSum and midSum <= rightSum.

leftSum <= midSum rearranges to prefix[j] >= 2*prefix[i]; midSum <= rightSum rearranges to 2*prefix[j] <= prefix[n]+prefix[i]. For a fixed i these are just two thresholds on prefix[j], so for each i find the smallest valid j and the largest valid j and count the range.

Since prefix sums are non-decreasing (values are non-negative), as i increases both threshold boundaries only move forward -- so sweep i from small to large while advancing two pointers (lowJ, highJ) forward, never resetting them, add max(0, highJ-lowJ+1) to the running total each time, and take the total modulo 1e9+7.

edge_inputs.description: statement example one, three equal elements
statement example two, several good splits
statement example three, no good split exists
minimum length of three, all zeros
trailing zeros can extend the right part for free without changing its sum
one huge element dominates, forcing it to be the right part only
strictly increasing values make an early split impossible
all zeros throughout, every split is a good split

stress.description: random non-negative values up to the max length and value cap
